title: Freestanding Ladder Bookcase
summary: A leaning-ladder bookcase where every joint is a 10° angle. The angle is the design — cut it consistently and the shelves float between the legs.
description:
A ladder bookcase looks casual but it's really a lesson in one repeated angle. Everything leans back at 10°, so almost every cut on this build is a 10° miter — the trim pieces between the shelves, the top and bottom trim, the stretcher at the back. Set your saw once and leave it there.

The clever bit is how the shelves are held: the legs are built up from a 1x2 spine with short 1x2 spacers nailed on at even gaps, and the shelves simply drop into the slots those spacers leave. Get the spacer gaps consistent and the shelves sit level and locked without any fussy joinery. Get them uneven and the shelves rock.

Because it leans rather than stands plumb, this piece must be secured to the wall — a leaning bookcase loaded with books is a tip-over waiting for a curious kid or a bumped ladder. Build it, then anchor it.

tools.note: You're making dozens of 10° angled cuts — a miter saw set to 10° and left there is what keeps them identical.
The spacers and trim are glued and pinned with brad nails.
For predrilling and screwing the 2x2 feet and stretcher.
Check the shelves are level as you set them — the leaning frame makes 'looks right' unreliable.

materials.name: 1x12, 8 ft
materials.note: Shelves — you can get two 48" shelves out of one 8-ft board if you cut them slightly under.

materials.name: 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Leg spines, spacers, and all the trim.

materials.name: 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Feet, leg bases, and the rear stretcher.

materials.name: Wall anchor / anti-tip hardware
materials.note: Not optional on a leaning bookcase. Anchor it into a stud.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 10°, parallel to each other.
Both ends at 10°, parallel. These set the shelf slots — cut them all identical.
Short-point to short-point; ends at 10° NOT parallel. Cut to fit the measured gap between the top legs.
Short-point to short-point; ends at 10° NOT parallel.
Short-point to short-point; both ends mitered at 10°.

steps.title: Cut all the leg trim at 10°
steps.body:
Start by cutting the four outer leg spines to 68-1/2", with both ends at 10° and parallel to each other. Then cut the inner trim pieces, all at 10° on both ends: four bottom-trim spacers at 6", sixteen between-shelf spacers at 13-1/8", and four top-trim spacers at 4-1/2".

Do this with the saw locked at one 10° setting so every angle matches. This is the batch of cuts that defines the whole piece.

steps.title: Build the two ladder legs
steps.body: Nail the inner spacer pieces to an outer spine, leaving a consistent 3/4" gap between all the boards — that gap plus the board width leaves slightly over 1-1/2" of open slot at the top of each stack, which is where a shelf will land. Use glue and 1-1/4" nails. Build both legs identical; any difference in gap spacing shows up as crooked shelves.

steps.title: Cut and fit the shelves
steps.body:
Cut five shelves at 48" (or slightly under, so you can get two out of one 8-ft board). Fit each shelf into the slots left between the inner spacers, centering them side to side on the legs.

Nail through the outer legs into the shelf ends with 2" nails and glue. Check each shelf is level before you commit the nails — on a leaning frame your eye will lie to you.

steps.title: Add the inside top and lower rails
steps.body:
Measure the actual distance between the legs at the top and cut the two upper rails to fit — both ends mitered at 10° but NOT parallel, roughly 23" long, 22-1/2" short-point to short-point. Nail in place with 1-1/4" nails and glue. Do the same for the lower inside rails.

Measuring the real gap rather than trusting the drawing matters here, because small errors in shelf placement change the span.

steps.title: Add the 2x2 tops and feet
steps.body: Cut two 2x2 pieces at 10" for the tops, predrill from the outside of the legs into the ends of the 2x2, and screw them on keeping the outside edges flush. Then cut two more 2x2s at 10" for the leg bases: keep the inside edge flush and line the bottom inside corner of the 2x2 up with the bottom inside edge of the legs.

steps.title: Tie the legs together with the stretcher
steps.body: Measure from the midpoint of the two base pieces and cut the rear stretcher to fit — roughly 43-1/4" short-point to short-point, both ends mitered at 10°. Predrill and screw it in place to lock the two legs into one rigid frame.

steps.title: Finish and anchor to the wall
steps.body:
Fill nail holes, sand everything smooth, and finish as desired.

Then anchor it. A leaning bookcase relies on the wall to stay upright — screw the top rail or a bracket into a stud with anti-tip hardware before you load a single book onto it.

images.alt: A leaning ladder-style bookcase with five shelves made from pine
